title: Memory Allocation
breadcrumbs: Development Considerations for Performance and Scalability
text: Memory Allocation Real memory (internal to the processor) can be accessed much more quickly than virtual memory (on disk). Falling back on virtual memory (that is, swapping) must therefore be kept to a minimum. To avoid excessive swapping, you can: Allocate sufficient internal memory—set the appropriate operating system parameters for internal memory allocation. Keep internal memory allocation to a minimum—select the appropriate form type, such as R (Record form) and L (Limited form). Performance gains can sometimes be achieved using the form property 'Keep data in memory' to reduce I/O on the tables, for example, for frequently referenced code lists. Release internal memory as soon as possible—for example, you can: Use the discard instruction in setocc loops Use the C option for the print instruction Define transactions of minimum size Related Links discard Remove one or more occurrences from the component and the hitlist. print Activate printing.
